message.content: ssh to cerebro.  Can you determine what type of SSDs it has?

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Determine SSD types on cerebro server

input.command: ssh cerebro 'lsblk -d -o NAME,ROTA,SIZE,MODEL,TRAN 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; for d in /sys/block/*/queue/rotational; do dev=$(echo $d | cut -d/ -f4); rota=$(cat $d); echo "$dev rota=$rota"; done; echo "---"; ls /sys/class/nvme/ 2>/dev/null'
input.description: SSH to cerebro and inspect block devices

content.text:
Cerebro has **4× WD_BLACK SN850X NVMe SSDs**, each 2 TB:

| Device | Model | Size | Transport |
|--------|-------|------|-----------|
| nvme0n1 | WD_BLACK SN850X 2000GB | 1.8 TiB | NVMe |
| nvme1n1 | WD_BLACK SN850X HS 2000GB | 1.8 TiB | NVMe |
| nvme2n1 | WD_BLACK SN850X 2000GB | 1.8 TiB | NVMe |
| nvme3n1 | WD_BLACK SN850X 2000GB | 1.8 TiB | NVMe |

All four are non-rotational (`ROTA=0`) and presented over NVMe. One unit (`nvme1n1`) is the "HS" variant (heatsink SKU); the other three are the bare variant. They're assembled into `md0` (a Linux software RAID array). Want me to dig deeper — RAID level, firmware versions, SMART health, or per-drive temperatures?

input.command: ssh cerebro 'for d in /dev/nvme0n1 /dev/nvme1n1 /dev/nvme2n1 /dev/nvme3n1; do echo "=== $d ==="; sudo smartctl -H -A "$d" 2>&1 | grep -E "(SMART overall|Model Number|Serial|Firmware|Temperature:|Available Spare|Percentage Used|Data Units|Power On|Power Cycles|Unsafe Shutdowns|Media and Data|Error Information Log Entries|Critical Warning)"; done'
input.description: Check SMART health on all four NVMe drives

input.command: ssh cerebro 'which smartctl; sudo -n smartctl -H /dev/nvme0n1 2>&1 | head -20'
input.description: Check smartctl availability and passwordless sudo

input.command: ssh cerebro 'which nvme; sudo -n nvme smart-log /dev/nvme0n1 2>&1 | head -30'
input.description: Check nvme-cli availability and try smart-log

content.text: Neither `smartmontools` nor `nvme-cli` is installed. A small amount of NVMe health data is exposed via sysfs without them — let me grab what's there first.

input.command: ssh cerebro 'for n in /sys/class/nvme/nvme*; do echo "=== $n ==="; echo -n "model: "; cat $n/model 2>/dev/null; echo -n "firmware: "; cat $n/firmware_rev 2>/dev/null; echo -n "serial: "; cat $n/serial 2>/dev/null; echo -n "state: "; cat $n/state 2>/dev/null; for hwmon in $n/hwmon*; do for tin in $hwmon/temp*_input; do label=${tin%_input}_label; echo "  temp $(cat $label 2>/dev/null || basename $tin): $(awk "{printf \"%.1f C\", \$1/1000}" $tin)"; done; done; done'
input.description: Pull NVMe identity and temps from sysfs

aiTitle: Determine SSD types on cerebro server

content.text:
Here's what sysfs gives without extra tooling:

| Device | Model | Firmware | State | Temp |
|--------|-------|----------|-------|------|
| nvme0 | WD_BLACK SN850X 2000GB | 620361WD | live | 46.9 °C |
| nvme1 | WD_BLACK SN850X HS 2000GB | 620361WD | live | 39.9 °C |
| nvme2 | WD_BLACK SN850X 2000GB | 620361WD | live | 46.9 °C |
| nvme3 | WD_BLACK SN850X 2000GB | 620361WD | live | 47.9 °C |

All four are `live`, on matching firmware, and well within NVMe thermal range. But the interesting wear/error metrics — `percentage_used`, `available_spare`, `media_errors`, `unsafe_shutdowns`, `power_on_hours` — live in the SMART log, which needs `nvme-cli` or `smartmontools`. Neither is installed.

Want me to `sudo apt install nvme-cli` (small, ~200 KB, no service)? Or do you prefer to install it yourself and re-run the check?

message.content: I installed that.  Retry.  What do you think of the temperatures of these SSDs?

content.text:
All four drives are essentially pristine — zero critical warnings, 100% spare, 0% wear, zero media errors, zero error log entries, never thermally throttled (T1/T2 transition counts all 0, warning/critical temp time all 0).

| Drive | Temp | Power-on h | TBW | Notes |
|-------|------|-----------|-----|-------|
| nvme0 | 47 °C | 6039 | 0.37 TB | bare |
| nvme1 | 40 °C | 5811 | 0.36 TB | **HS** (heatsink) |
| nvme2 | 47 °C | 5578 | **2.02 TB** | bare — ~6× the writes of the others |
| nvme3 | 48 °C | 5688 | 0.36 TB | bare |

One non-thermal thing worth flagging: nvme2 has done ~2 TB of writes vs ~360 GB on the others. If you expected the four to wear evenly (e.g., md0 RAID10/5/6 with symmetric I/O), that asymmetry suggests nvme2 isn't in the same balanced role — possibly outside the array, or the array isn't striping/mirroring the way I'd guess. Worth a `cat /proc/mdstat` if that's surprising.

**On the temperatures specifically: they're good, with room to spare.**

- **40-48 °C is the comfortable middle of the SN850X's operating range** (0-70 °C spec, warning threshold typically 80 °C, critical ~85 °C). Most NVMes idle 35-50 °C and climb to 55-70 °C under sustained write load.
- **The drives have never been thermally stressed.** `Warning Temperature Time = 0`, `Critical Composite Temperature Time = 0`, and zero T1/T2 thermal-management transitions across ~5500-6000 power-on hours. They've never throttled.
- **The 7-8 °C gap between the HS drive (40 °C) and the bare drives (47-48 °C) is exactly the heatsink delta you'd expect** — the bare ones aren't running hot, they're just less aggressively cooled.
- **One mild caveat:** these temps are at light load (writes have been modest — even nvme2 has only done 2 TB lifetime). The SN850X is known to climb fast under sustained sequential writes; the bare drives starting at 47 °C have less headroom than the HS one if you ever push them hard (e.g., a long rebuild, large dataset ingest). If you plan heavy sustained-write workloads, the 7 °C margin the HS gives you is meaningful — but for current usage you're well clear of trouble.

Net: healthy, cool enough, no action needed.

input.command: ssh cerebro 'cat /proc/mdstat; echo "---"; sudo -n mdadm --detail /dev/md0 2>&1; echo "---"; lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,TYPE,FSTYPE,MOUNTPOINT,MODEL'

aiTitle: Determine SSD types on cerebro server

content.text:
Asymmetry fully explained — but with a finding worth flagging.

**Layout:**
- **md0 (root, ext4, 5.5 TiB) = RAID0 across nvme0/nvme1/nvme3** — three drives striped, no redundancy.
- **nvme2 is not in the array.** It's a separate disk holding a Windows install: EFI (100 MB, also mounted as Linux's `/boot/efi`), MSR (16 MB), Windows C: (983 GB NTFS), Windows Recovery (735 MB NTFS).

So you have a dual-boot machine. nvme2's 2 TB of lifetime writes is Windows-side accumulation (pagefile, Windows Update, browser cache, etc.), accounted separately from the Linux filesystem. The three Linux drives each show ~360 GB — that's ~1.08 TB of total user writes to `/` striped evenly across the three array members, which matches RAID0 chunk distribution.

**The thing worth raising:** your root filesystem is RAID0 across three NVMes. That's a deliberate performance-over-resilience trade — fine if it's intentional, but it does mean a single-drive failure on nvme0, nvme1, *or* nvme3 takes the whole OS install with it, and the failure probability is roughly 3× a single drive. The SN850X is reliable (0% wear, zero errors across 5500-6000 hours here), so this isn't urgent — just confirming you know the topology is "fast, not redundant." Backups become the only line of defense for `/`. If that wasn't intended, the array would need to be rebuilt as RAID10 (across 4 drives, but nvme2 is occupied by Windows) or RAID5.

Nothing actionable on temps/health — wanted to surface the redundancy posture in case it's news.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about XMP support and DDR5 6000 on the MSI PRO Z890-P WIFI:

## XMP Support

XMP Profiles in the MSI BIOS are MSI OC LAB optimized and can be easily enabled with auto power settings for optimized memory speed and stability. The motherboard supports this through preset XMP profiles to automatically overclock compatible DDR memory for optimal performance.

## DDR5 Memory Support

The board supports DDR5 Memory, Dual Channel DDR5 9200+ MT/s (OC), which means it can support memory speeds well beyond DDR5 6000 when overclocked.

## Enabling XMP in BIOS

To enable XMP for DDR5 6000 memory:
1. Enter the BIOS (Click BIOS X interface)
2. Navigate to the memory/overclocking settings
3. Choose from preset XMP profiles to automatically overclock compatible DDR memory for optimal performance.
4. Select your memory's XMP profile and enable it with the auto power settings

The motherboard also features Memory Extension Mode can combine XMP profiles to maximize memory frequency, enabling users to effortlessly discover the best configuration based on their requirements.

The MSI PRO Z890-P WIFI should handle DDR5 6000 without any issues, as its maximum supported speed far exceeds this specification.

Based on the search results, I found information about Kingston FURY DDR5 memory specifications, though the results don't contain a specific datasheet for the exact model KF560C32-48. However, I can provide you with details about similar Kingston FURY Beast DDR5 models:

## Kingston FURY Beast DDR5 Specifications

Kingston FURY Beast DDR5 features Intel® XMP 3.0 or AMD EXPO™, two new DDR5 overclock specs that include customizable profiles for speeds and timings.

**Speed Capabilities:**
DDR5-6000 is available as a speed option, with increasingly attractive pricing making DDR5-6000 a safe bet for this generation and the next.

**XMP Profiles:**
Based on similar Kingston FURY models in the search results:
- Modules are tested to run at DDR5-6000 at a low latency timing of 36-38-38 at 1.35V
- JEDEC standard latency is DDR5-4800 timing of 40-39-39 at 1.1V

**Features:**
Kingston FURY Beast DDR5 offers overclock specs for Intel® XMP 3.0 and AMD EXPO™ with customizable profiles for speeds and timings, and features on-die ECC for improved stability at extreme speeds, dual 32-bit subchannels for increased efficiency, and an on-module power management integrated circuit.

For the specific KF560C32-48 model datasheet, you may want to check Kingston's official product page or datasheet repository directly.

content.text:
Yes — confirmed, and the uplift on the table is significant.

**What you have:**
- 4× Kingston FURY Beast DDR5 `KF560C32-48` (48 GB each = 192 GB total) — decoded as: `5`=DDR5, `60`=**6000 MT/s** rated, `C32`=CL32, `-48`=48 GB DIMM
- Motherboard: MSI PRO Z890-P WIFI (supports DDR5 up to 9200+ MT/s OC)

**The feature your DIMMs have but your BIOS isn't using:** Kingston FURY Beast DDR5 modules ship with **Intel XMP 3.0** and **AMD EXPO** profiles in their SPD. On your Intel Z890 platform the relevant one is XMP 3.0 — a one-click BIOS toggle that applies the manufacturer's tested speed/timing/voltage profile. Without it, the system trains to the JEDEC fallback (which is exactly what dmidecode showed):

| | Current (JEDEC default) | XMP profile (rated) |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | 4400 MT/s | **6000 MT/s** |
| Voltage | 1.1 V | 1.35 V |
| Latency | JEDEC (loose) | CL32-38-38 (typical for this SKU) |
| Bandwidth | ~70 GB/s | ~96 GB/s |

That's a **~36% data-rate increase** — meaningful for anything memory-bandwidth-bound (compiles, large compressed I/O, virtualization, LLM inference on CPU, scientific workloads).

**How to enable:** Reboot → enter MSI BIOS (Del key) → switch to Advanced mode (F7) → OC section → set "Extreme Memory Profile (XMP)" to **Profile 1**. Save and exit. The board will retrain memory on the next boot (one or two slow boots are normal as it does this).

**One honest caveat for your specific config:** you're running **4 DIMMs, dual-rank, on a single Intel platform** (2 DPC, 2R). This is the hardest case for the DDR5 memory controller. Many 4×48 GB DDR5-6000 kits on Z890 train fine with XMP, but a non-trivial fraction land at 5200-5600 MT/s instead of the full 6000 — or boot fine but throw occasional instability under sustained load. If you see boot loops, "memory training failed" messages, or instability after enabling XMP, the fallback ladder is: try Profile 2 (often a tamer XMP), or use MSI's "Memory Try It!" presets to step down to 5600/5200. Even 5200 MT/s would be a ~18% gain over 4400, so it's still worth it. The board also has "Memory Context Restore" enabled by default on recent BIOSes, which skips re-training on subsequent boots once a stable profile is found — so the slow-boot pain is one-time.

Bottom line: yes, your RAM is rated for 6000 MT/s with XMP profiles you've never enabled, and you're currently leaving ~36% of memory bandwidth on the table. Whether that matters depends on your workloads.

Sources:
- [Kingston FURY Beast DDR5 product page](https://www.kingston.com/en/memory/gaming/kingston-fury-beast-ddr5-memory)
- [MSI PRO Z890-P WIFI motherboard page](https://www.msi.com/Motherboard/PRO-Z890-P-WIFI)

content: Audited cerebro's hardware health: 4× WD_BLACK SN850X NVMes (healthy, cool, root on 3-drive RAID0; Windows on the 4th) and 192 GB Kingston FURY Beast DDR5-6000 running at 4400 MT/s because XMP is off. Next: enable XMP Profile 1 in the MSI BIOS. (disable recaps in /config)
